quiz.title: Algebra 2: Unit 9 Quiz 9
quiz.subtitle: 9.9: Binomial Probability
quiz.subject: Mathematics
quiz.topic: Calculating probabilities for sequences of independent Bernoulli trials

questions.question: A fair coin is flipped 5 times. What is the probability of getting exactly 3 heads?
A basketball player makes 80% of her free throws. If she shoots 4 free throws, what is the probability she makes exactly 3?
A multiple-choice quiz has 5 questions, each with 4 options. If a student guesses randomly on all questions, what is the probability they get exactly 2 correct?
A coin is weighted so that $P(\text{heads}) = 0.3$. If the coin is flipped 4 times, what is the probability of getting exactly 2 heads?
A seed has a 90% germination rate. If you plant 10 seeds, what is the probability that exactly 8 will grow? Round to four decimal places.
A bag contains 3 red marbles and 7 blue marbles. If a marble is picked and replaced 3 times, what is the probability of picking exactly 1 red marble?
What is the probability of getting at least 1 tail in 3 coin flips?
A factory finds that 1% of its products are defective. If 5 items are chosen at random, what is the probability that none are defective?
If you roll a standard die 4 times, what is the probability of rolling exactly two 6s?
A machine produces parts with a 5% defect rate. If 6 parts are tested, what is the probability that at least 5 are good?

scoreMessages.90: Master level! You navigated the binomial formula perfectly — exactly k successes, at least k successes, complement problems, weighted coins, free throws, and quality control.
scoreMessages.70: Great job. Key reminders: (1) $P(k) = C(n,k) \cdot p^k \cdot q^{n-k}$, (2) $q = 1 - p$ always, (3) For 'at least' problems, add probabilities or use the complement, (4) $C(n,k) = \frac{n!}{k!(n-k)!}$.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing. Focus on: (1) Identifying $n$, $k$, $p$, and $q$ before plugging into the formula, (2) Computing $C(n,k)$ correctly, (3) For 'at least 1' problems, use $1 - P(0)$ — it's faster, (4) 'With replacement' means $p$ stays constant — binomial applies.
scoreMessages.0: Review binomial probability: (1) $P(k) = C(n,k) \cdot p^k \cdot q^{n-k}$ where $n$ = trials, $k$ = successes, $p$ = P(success), $q = 1-p$, (2) $C(n,k)$ counts how many ways to choose which $k$ trials are successes, (3) The trials must be independent with constant $p$, (4) For 'at least' problems: add $P(k) + P(k+1) + ... + P(n)$ or use $1 - P(\text{complement})$.
